file_name: Muthukumaravel et al. - 2026 - Intelligent skin disease detection and classification using convolutional neural networks on dermosc.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and implements an automated skin disease classification system using Deep Learning (DL) methods, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). It utilizes the HAM10000 dataset, applies Transfer Learning with a pre-trained CNN architecture for feature extraction, and uses the Adam optimizer with categorical cross-entropy loss for training. The system is designed to classify skin diseases from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes and implements an automated skin disease classification system using Deep Learning (CNNs) on the HAM10000 dataset. It details specific methods including preprocessing, transfer learning, and the Adam optimizer, and presents original experimental results with performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices for models like EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, and VGG16.

title: Intelligent Skin Disease Detection and Classification Using Convolutional Neural Networks on Dermoscopic Images
journal: AVE Trends in Intelligent Health Letters
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated skin disease classification system using Deep Learning to address the subjectivity and time constraints of traditional dermatological diagnosis. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermoscopic images of seven pigmented skin lesion categories, and applied preprocessing steps including resizing, normalization, and noise reduction. The methodology employed Transfer Learning with three pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network architectures: ResNet50, VGG16, and EfficientNetB0, optimized using the Adam optimizer with categorical cross-entropy loss. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices across all seven classes. The results demonstrated that EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest performance metrics, particularly excelling in accuracy and F1-score compared to the other models. The authors conclude that EfficientNetB0 is the most effective model for automated skin lesion classification and suggest its deployment in web or mobile applications for early detection and clinical decision support.

all_task_types.target: Seven pigmented skin lesion categories (NV, ME, BK, BC, AK, VL, DF)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin diseases using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma) using Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB0) with categorical cross-entropy loss. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public, clinically verified dataset containing 10,015 dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions. The images were acquired under standardized conditions and labels were verified by expert dermatologists, with many diagnoses confirmed via histopathology.

use.training: Used to train the CNN models (ResNet50, VGG16, EfficientNetB0) after preprocessing (resizing, normalization, noise reduction).
use.testing: Used to evaluate model performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest performance metrics across most classes compared to ResNet50 and VGG16. The confusion matrix for EfficientNetB0 showed high diagonal values, indicating correct classification, with minimal misclassification between visually similar lesions. Confusion was noted between Melanoma (ME) and Melanocytic nevi (NV) due to visual similarity.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer Learning with weights pre-trained on ImageNet

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Pre-trained CNN architectures used for feature extraction via Transfer Learning

training_details.normalization: Min-Max Scaling (pixel values scaled from 0-255 to 0-1)

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: Resizing all dermoscopy images to a static input dimension of 224 × 224 pixels using spatial normalization.
Pixel Normalization: Applying Min-Max Scaling to scale pixel intensities from the range (0, 255) to (0, 1) to improve numerical stability and convergence.
Color Correction/Normalization: Performing color normalization to address variations in color across images.
Noise Reduction: Applying noise reduction techniques to remove unwanted artifacts and improve image quality.
conclusions: The proposed automated skin disease classification system using Deep Learning (CNNs) effectively addresses the subjectivity, time constraints, and expertise limitations of traditional dermatological diagnosis.

EfficientNetB0 demonstrated superior performance compared to ResNet50 and VGG16 across almost all seven pigmented skin lesion classes in the HAM10000 dataset, achieving the highest accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores.
EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest accuracy for Melanocytic nevi (0.987), Benign keratosis (0.993), and Dermatofibroma (0.992), and the highest precision for Melanocytic nevi (0.991).
The model showed robust performance with minimal misclassifications, although some confusion remained between visually similar lesions, particularly between Melanoma (ME) and Melanocytic nevi (NV).
Transfer Learning with pre-trained CNN architectures allows for effective feature extraction and good performance even with limited medical data.

The approach has significant diagnostic potential for dermatologists and can be deployed in online or mobile applications to facilitate early identification of skin diseases and improve accessibility to clinical decision support.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits a natural class imbalance with a high incidence of benign lesions (e.g., NV: 6788 images) compared to malignant or rare classes (e.g., DF: 112 images), potentially biasing model performance towards dominant classes.
No external validation was performed; the model was only trained and tested on the HAM10000 dataset, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported to validate the significance of performance differences between EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, and VGG16.

Data leakage risks are present as the paper does not explicitly describe the train/validation/test split methodology or confirm that patient-level separation was maintained, which is critical for HAM10000.

No data augmentation techniques were employed, relying solely on preprocessing (resizing, normalization, noise reduction), which may limit the model's robustness to variations in real-world dermoscopic images.

Performance was notably lower for minority classes such as Vascular Lesions (VL) and Dermatofibroma (DF), with EfficientNetB0 achieving an F1-score of only 0.870 for VL, indicating challenges with rare or visually similar classes.
The paper claims potential for deployment in web or mobile applications but provides no evidence of computational efficiency, latency, or resource constraints analysis required for such deployment.

Confusion between Melanoma (ME) and Melanocytic Nevi (NV) was noted due to visual similarity, highlighting a persistent diagnostic challenge that the model did not fully resolve despite high overall accuracy.
